query: anatomy of sublingual and submandibular spaces and what divides them

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sublingual Gland -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The sublingual glands drain into the submandibular lymph nodes. The submandibular lymphatics comprise 3 to 6 nodes, which are located in the submaxillary triangle, beneath the body of the mandible. The nodes are palpable on the superficial surface of the submandibular gland. Malignant tumors may drain into these regional lymph nodes, requiring more extensive neck dissection for the complete treatment of cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Submandibular Triangle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The submandibular triangle is an important anatomic landmark located underneath the body of the mandible. The majority of the anatomic space bounded by the submandibular triangle consists of the submandibular gland. The submandibular gland is the second largest salivary gland in the body (the parotid gland is the largest). In addition to housing the submandibular gland, a vast array of nerves, arteries, veins, and lymphatics course within the boundaries of the submandibular triangle. The complexity of the anatomy within the submandibular triangle makes for important clinical implications when performing surgery in this region. This article will focus on the anatomy contained within the borders of the submandibular triangle as well as the clinical significance and implications of surgery in this area. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Submandibular Gland Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The paired submandibular glands lie within the submandibular triangle. This space is bounded superiorly by the inferior border of the mandible, posteroinferiorly by the posterior belly of the digastric, and anteroinferiorly by the anterior belly of the digastric (see Image . Superficial Neck Anatomy). The mylohyoid forms the deep margin of the triangle, while the superficial boundary is the platysma. The gland curves around the posterior border of the mylohyoid, so that the deep lobe projects into the floor of the mouth and lies adjacent to the hyoglossus muscle medially (see Image . Lateral View of the Salivary Glands).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Submandibular Gland -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The submandibular gland is the second largest of the 3 main salivary glands, including the parotid and sublingual glands. The submandibular glands are paired with major salivary glands in the submandibular triangle (see Image. Submandibular Triangle). The glands have a superficial and deep lobe separated by the mylohyoid muscle [1] . The Wharton duct, the submandibular gland’s primary excretory duct, drains into the oral cavity at the sublingual caruncle. The sublingual caruncle is a papilla located medial to the sublingual gland and lateral to each side of the frenulum linguae [1] . The submandibular gland produces approximately 70% of the saliva in the unstimulated state. However, the parotid gland’s saliva production predominates once the salivary glands become stimulated (see Image. The Mouth, Dissection). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sublingual Gland -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The sublingual gland is the smallest of the three major salivary glands [1] , which also include the parotid and submandibular glands. The sublingual gland lies between the muscles of the floor of the oral cavity, which include the geniohyoid muscle, hyoglossus muscle medially, and the mylohyoid muscle inferiorly. The mandible borders the sublingual glands laterally [2] .

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Submandibular Gland Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: On the medial surface of the submandibular gland, the submandibular ganglion connects to the lingual nerve and provides parasympathetic innervation. The ganglion must be separated from the gland during resection to preserve lingual nerve function (see Image . Maxillary and Mandibular Nerve Distribution). The hypoglossal nerve courses deep and inferior to the lingual nerve and may be encountered during excision, requiring careful preservation (see Image . Muscles, Arteries, and Nerves of the Neck).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sublingual Gland -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The sublingual glands receive their primary blood supply from the sublingual and submental arteries, which are branches of the lingual artery and facial artery, respectively. These arteries are both branches of the external carotid artery. The sublingual vein drains into the lingual vein, which then flows into the internal jugular system.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Sublingual Gland
retrieved_docs.content: The sublingual gland receives blood from the submental and sublingual arteries , which are branches of the lingual and facial arteries. While deoxygenated blood is carried away by veins parallel to the arteries mentioned above, drainage occurs predominantly through the submandibular lymph nodes [17] .

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Submandibular Gland Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The submandibular gland receives its primary vascular supply from the facial artery, via the sublingual and submental branches, with venous drainage through the facial vein. The lingual artery and sublingual vein provide additional contribution. The facial vein runs across the superficial surface of the gland, crossing the posterior belly of the digastric muscle before draining into the common facial vein and then the internal jugular vein. The facial artery originates from the external carotid artery and follows a tortuous course along the deep aspect of the gland (see Image . Veins, Arteries, and Nerves of the Face and Scalp)

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sublingual Gland -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ducts of Rivinus, a group of excretory ducts, drain the sublingual gland. The largest sublingual gland excretory duct called the sublingual duct of Bartholin joins Wharton’s duct near the sublingual caruncle. The sublingual caruncle is a papilla located medial to the sublingual gland and lateral to the lingual frenulum [2] . The sublingual gland contributes approximately 5% of saliva in the oral cavity [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Structure and Function -- Anatomical Location
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid gland (PG) is the largest of the three major salivary glands. It is located between the sternocleidomastoid muscle and the masseter, extending from the mastoid tip to just below the angle of the mandible. A small tail projects from the inferior edge of the gland, separated by the submandibular space only by the stylomandibular ligament. From superficial to deep, the gland is superficial to the facial nerve, an area of crucial surgical relevance, followed by the retromandibular vein and the external carotid artery. Stensen’s duct , the main excretory duct of the PG, projects from the anterior portion of the gland over the masseter. In its trajectory, it pierces the buccinator muscle to open into the oral cavity at the level of the buccal mucosa of the second maxillary molar [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Submandibular Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The blood vessels and lymphatics that course through the submandibular triangle are significant landmarks to note for surgical incision into the triangle. The facial artery, being the fourth branch of the external carotid artery, courses underneath the posterior belly of the digastric and sits behind the submandibular gland in the triangle. After the artery passes posterior to the submandibular gland, it courses superiorly across the inferior border of the mandible. The submental artery and mylohyoid artery also course deep to the submandibular gland within the triangle. The submandibular lymph nodes follow the veins in the triangle and drain many of the inferior portions of the oral cavity. The facial vein runs superficially to the submandibular gland and eventually joins the anterior branch of the retromandibular vein, forming the common facial vein that drains into the internal jugular vein. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Submandibular Triangle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Superficial to deep dissection of the submandibular triangle involves passing through the skin, subcutaneous fat, superficial cervical fascia, platysma muscle, and deep cervical fascia before reaching the submandibular gland. The majority of the space within the triangle is taken up by the submandibular gland that helps produce salivary fluid secreted into the oral cavity. Additionally, the gland helps to facilitate lymph drainage from the lower gums, tongue, the floor of the mouth, and tonsils into the 3-6 lymph nodes contained within the triangle which lie adjacent to the submandibular gland. Unlike the parotid gland, the submandibular gland contains no intraglandular lymph nodes. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Structure and Function -- Anatomical Location
retrieved_docs.content: The sublingual gland (SLG) lies beneath the mucosa of the floor of the mouth and superior to the mylohyoid muscle (between the mandible and genioglossus muscles). Medially, between the base of the tongue and the sublingual gland, the submandibular duct, and the sublingual nerve can be found. Rather than having one main duct, it contains a series of short ducts that project directly into the floor of the mouth, the ducts of Rivinus , and a common duct, known as Bartholin's duct that connects with the submandibular gland's duct at the sublingual caruncula [4] [5] [6] [5] .

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is morphologically associated with الورم السرطاني (cancerous tumor) and is mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas, indicating that this anatomic variant is a subset of pancreatic cancer. Metastatic involvement of lymph nodes in the head and neck region is captured by several entities: metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of the head and neck, metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of the head and neck, and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck. All three are classified as secondary malignancies, each “mapped to” the secondary‑malignancy group and described by inverse‑is‑a relationships to الورم السرطاني (cancerous tumor) or, for the squamous cell type, to سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Digestive System organizes neoplasms—including neoplasms of unspecified nature or uncertain behavior, malignant lip cancer, pancreatic cancer, and metastatic malignant neoplasms such as those to the sublingual gland—under broader categories of malignant tumors. Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland is defined as a secondary malignancy (Arabic “النقيلة”) and classified as a malignant tumor of the sublingual gland. Metastatic involvement of cervical and head‑and‑neck lymph nodes (metastatic carcinoma, metastatic adenocarcinoma, metastatic squamous‑cell carcinoma, metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck) is mapped to the secondary‑malignancy subgroup “نقائل إلى الرقبة” within the non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ. These entities are inverse instances of the general concept “الورم السرطاني” and share morphological descriptors such as “سرطانة غدية نقيلية” (metastatic glandular carcinoma) and “سرطانة

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 796
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان) and is linked through a “has relationship” connection to the term النقيلة. The entity Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant also has a “has relationship” connection to النقيلة, indicating that the concept النقيلة encompasses both clearly metastatic lesions and neoplasms of indeterminate behavior. Furthermore, النقيلة is related by “has relationship” to سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس (metastatic cancer in the head of the pancreas), suggesting that النقيلة functions as a unifying descriptor for metastatic disease across different anatomical sites. Together, these relationships illustrate that النقيلة serves as a common link among various malignant and uncertain neoplasms, including those arising in the sublingual gland and the pancreatic head, highlighting the importance of recognizing metastatic potential in diverse tumor types for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2338
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is linked to the Arabic classification ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى (malignant secondary tumor in other specified sites), indicating that this metastatic lesion is coded as a secondary malignant neoplasm occurring in a defined location. The relationship inverse_isa between Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان (malignant tumor in the sublingual gland) shows that the broader category of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland encompasses the more specific entity of a malignant tumor arising in the sublingual gland. Additionally, the Arabic term سرطان الجلد الخبيث (malignant skin cancer) is also mapped from ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى, suggesting that malignant skin cancer shares the same coding reference as other secondary malignant neoplasms in specified sites. Together, these connections illustrate how metastatic lesions to the sublingual gland and primary malignant skin cancers are both classified under the umbrella of secondary malignant tumors in particular locations, underscoring the importance of precise terminology and coding for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and epidemiological tracking.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node and metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are both classified as secondary malignancies, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue in the head and neck region. In addition, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is directly linked to the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term “السرطان النقيلي,” which denotes metastatic cancer. Recognizing these entities as secondary malignancies and understanding their characteristic metastatic morphology are important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management of patients with head‑and‑neck cancer spread.
